# The Confluence of SDGs #4 and #5 — Fertility Rates as a Signal of Progress

The SDG framework for measuring progress in equitable and sustainable development requires frequent progress assessments through data analysis. A notable demographic trend has accompanied progress on gender equality and education — global fertility rates are declining. Reports show a drop from roughly five children per woman in 1965 to about 2.5 today. Researchers attribute this shift primarily to three factors: women's empowerment and access to education, declining child mortality, and the rising cost of raising children (Roser, [2014](#G)).
The relationship between women's education and lower fertility is well-established across many contexts. Jain ([1981](#H)) identified this pattern across twelve countries, and Martín ([1995](#J)) confirmed it across 26 demographic and health surveys, finding that higher education is consistently associated with lower fertility, later marriage, and greater contraceptive use — all indicators of women's reproductive autonomy. Another study found the same inverse relationship in China, and proposed that declining fertility rates can themselves serve as a novel indicator of progress toward SDGs #4 and #5 (Chen & Guo, [2022](#I)).  
At the individual level, a recent article from the New York Times found that women in the United States are delaying childbearing not out of indifference, but out of a desire for financial stability and readiness: "I want to be financially stable and in a place I can call my own... I saw my parents get stressed over money, and I don't want my kid to experience that." (Tavernise & Adelson, [2026](#K)). This reflects a broader shift in more developed nations; women exercising the informed, autonomous decision-making that the SDG framework was designed to enable. The global decline in fertility rates is less a cause for concern than a sign that decades of investment in gender equality and education are beginning to take hold, particularly in more developed nations across the globe.
